title: How Biological Catalysts Make Life Possible

imageCredit.creator: Matthias M.
imageCredit.license: Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 Unported / GNU Free Documentation License 1.2+

lesson.learn:
Enzymes are proteins that speed up chemical reactions without being used up themselves. They do this by lowering activation energy, which makes it easier for molecules to reach the transition state and turn into products. Because cells are full of reactions that need to happen quickly and safely, enzymes are central to metabolism.

What makes enzymes powerful is specificity. An enzyme's active site fits certain substrates better than others, so the cell can control which reactions happen and when. That precision is one reason life can run on chemistry instead of chaos.
lesson.simpler: Enzymes are helpers that make reactions happen faster. They do not get used up, so they can keep working again and again.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Enzymes speed up life by lowering activation energy and guiding reactions with high specificity.
lesson.deeperDive:
A good enzyme does more than just bind a substrate. It positions molecules, stabilizes temporary states, and can even help break or form chemical bonds in a very controlled way. That is why the same general chemistry can proceed millions or billions of times faster in the presence of the right catalyst.

Enzymes are also regulated. Cells can increase or decrease enzyme activity, turn enzymes on and off, or separate them into different compartments. This lets metabolism stay responsive to changing conditions. Digestion, DNA copying, ATP production, and detoxification all depend on enzyme control.
lesson.funFact: Some enzymes are so efficient that they approach the theoretical speed limit for how fast a chemical reaction can happen.
references: Royal Society of Chemistry - Biological catalysts

people: Emil Fischer - Helped explain enzyme specificity
Anselme Payen - Isolated one of the first enzymes
Arthur Kornberg - Advanced enzyme biology in DNA replication
Daniel Koshland - Contributed to induced fit theory
Jens Christian Skou - Studied membrane enzymes
places: Rockefeller University - Major enzyme research site
The Salk Institute - Strong biomedical research center
Cambridge University - Historic site in biochemistry
The Broad Institute - Modern center for biology and chemistry
years: 2000s - Structural biology reveals active sites in detail
concepts: Catalyst - A substance that speeds up a reaction
Active site - The region where binding and chemistry happen
Activation energy - The barrier a reaction must overcome
Substrate - The molecule an enzyme acts on
Kinetics - The study of reaction speed
